summary: Uncle Bob uses IBM Watson's infamous 'Toronto' gaffe from Jeopardy to challenge the optimistic predictions about driverless cars. While Watson dramatically outperformed humans overall, it made a fundamentally inhuman error by naming Toronto as a US city—a mistake no reasonably educated person would make. He argues that driverless cars will similarly outperform humans statistically while inevitably making catastrophic errors that no human would make. The post culminates in a hypothetical courtroom scene where a computer must explain why it killed a child, with the chilling implication that its 'reason' may be as incomprehensible as Watson's Toronto answer.
key_points: AI systems can dramatically outperform humans on aggregate metrics while making errors that are fundamentally alien and incomprehensible to human reasoning
Computers always have discrete, unambiguous reasons for their decisions, but those reasons may be utterly divorced from human logic or common sense
The inevitability of driverless car tragedies will demand explanations that computers cannot provide in humanly satisfying terms
Statistical safety improvements don't address the problem of individually inexplicable catastrophic failures
No human would insist Toronto is a US city—such categorical errors reveal a fundamental gap in machine 'understanding'
money_quotes: I have one word for all these predictions: Toronto!
The mistakes that Watson made were not the kind of mistakes that a human would make.
Watson was, after all, a computer; and computers always have absolutely discrete and unambiguous reasons for what they do.
Whatever the reasons, the answer that Watson gave was supremely stupid.
No human of moderate intelligence and education would have made that mistake.
Indeed, any human who insisted on that answer, as Watson surely would have, might very well be deemed legally insane.
The car will have had a good reason to kill that child.
key_insight: AI systems that statistically outperform humans can still make catastrophically stupid errors that no human would make, and when those errors kill people, the computer's 'reasons' will be as incomprehensible as naming Toronto a US city.
